_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Sunflower County, MS. County seat: Indianola. Records survive reasonably well — no major fire or flood loss at the courthouse itself, though the 1927 flood affected the county. The primary record challenge is not loss but exclusion: the systematic underregistration of Black births, deaths, and marriages in a county that was 70-80% Black.

previous_courthouses.fate: County seat moved to Indianola in 1882.

records_destroyed: loose court papers from McNutt era
record_losses.description: The move from McNutt to Indianola resulted in some record loss, primarily loose papers. Bound volumes generally survived the transfer. The county's late development (created 1844) means the earliest records are not as old as in longer-established counties.
record_losses.narrative_impact: Sunflower County's records start relatively late (1844) compared to river-front counties. For ancestors before that date, the area was part of Bolivar County, and records must be sought there.
record_losses.source: Mississippi Department of Archives and History, Guide to County Records.

deeds.gaps: Minor gaps in the earliest deed books. Continuous from the 1850s.
deeds.repository: Sunflower County Chancery Clerk, Indianola; MDAH microfilm

wills_estates.completeness: Good — estate records from early county period
wills_estates.gaps: Limited pre-1850 records due to late county formation.

marriages.completeness: Good for white families; Black marriages documented from 1865
marriages.gaps: Pre-emancipation enslaved marriages not documented. Post-1865 Black marriage records exist but are less complete than white records.
marriages.repository: Sunflower County Circuit Clerk; MDAH microfilm

court_minutes.completeness: Good — chancery and circuit court minutes largely continuous

vital_records.completeness: Poor for rural Black population until 1930s. Fannie Lou Hamer's own birth date is uncertain in official records.
vital_records.repository: Mississippi State Department of Health; MDAH

substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential for tracking families. The 1870 and 1880 censuses are the first to document formerly enslaved individuals by name in Sunflower County.

substitute_sources.description: Labor contracts, marriage records, and complaint files for Sunflower County, 1865-1872.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Critical bridge for Black families. Sunflower County's overwhelmingly Black population makes these records essential.

substitute_sources.description: Some Sunflower County plantation records survive, including the Eastland plantation papers and Dockery Plantation records.
substitute_sources.repository: MDAH; university special collections; private holdings
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Plantation records document individual workers, labor arrangements, and family units. For ancestors who worked specific plantations, these records may be the most detailed source available.
substitute_sources.source: Mississippi Department of Archives and History.

narrative_hooks: Sunflower County's records start in 1844 — for ancestors before that date, the area was part of Bolivar County, and {ancestor_name}'s trail leads to a different courthouse.

The vital records system that should have documented {ancestor_name}'s birth was so unreliable for Black families that even Fannie Lou Hamer's own birth date is uncertain in official records — the system did not value the documentation of the people it exploited.

If {ancestor_name} worked a specific plantation in Sunflower County, plantation records — where they survive — may document the family in more detail than the county courthouse, because the planter tracked labor more carefully than the state tracked citizens.

The Freedmen's Bureau records for Sunflower County are the essential bridge between slavery and freedom for {ancestor_name}'s family — the first documents to record names, ages, family relationships, and former enslavers for the county's Black majority.
source_references: Mississippi Department of Archives and History. Guide to County Records. Jackson, MS.
National Archives and Records Administration. Records of the Bureau of Refugees, Freedmen, and Abandoned Lands (RG 105).
Mills, Kay. This Little Light of Mine: The Life of Fannie Lou Hamer. New York: Dutton, 1993.
